coredata.dc:title: A novel strategy for the preparation of multifunctional and robust polyhydroxyalkanoates/GQDs nanocomposites with UV-resistance, pH-responsive and antibacterial properties
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Qian
Xu, Pengwu
Lv, Dongxuan
Xu, Yunsheng
Zhang, Jingchen
Liu, Tianxi
Yang, Weijun
Ma, Piming
coredata.dc:description: Biosynthesized polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHAs) offer several compelling advantages, including biodegradability, biocompatibility, and suitability for use in environmentally friendly packaging materials. However, poor crystallization ability and lack of functionality (e.g., UV-resistance and antibacterial properties) significantly restrict the practical applications of PHAs. In this study, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) containing with rich amino groups, carboxy groups and conjugate structure were tailor-made as multifunctional nanofillers to prepare PHAs/GQDs composites. The GQDs not only exhibited excellent dispersion within the PHAs matrix at the nanoscale but also act as nucleating agents to dramatically increase the nucleation density of PHAs. Remarkably, the incorporation of just 0.5 wt% GQDs led to outstanding antibacterial performance (99.99%), UV resistance (90.50%), transparency (>80%) and mechanical properties in the PHAs composite. ​Moreover, these PHAs composites displayed strong fluorescence stability and demonstrated sensitivity to pH variations. Building upon these findings, we propose a novel concept for a wearable device capable of intelligently detecting changes in sweat pH during human exercise. This work presents a straightforward and effective method for preparing multifunctional GQDs, thereby expanding the potential applications of PHAs in the realm of green packaging and intelligent sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PHAs
Multifunctional GQDs
UV-resistance
pH-responsive
Antimicrobial activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence properties and pH responsiveness of PHAs/GQDs film

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the preparation procedure of GQDs and PHAs/GQDs films.

Structural and morphological characteristics of GQDs nanoparticles. (a) FTIR spectra, (b) AFM heights image, (c) 3D height images (c': height profiles measured along the line in b, c“: height distribution statistics), (d) XRD spectra, (e) Raman spectra, (f) XPS wide scan spectra, (g) XPS corresponding C1s, N1s, O1s core peaks.

Fluorescence properties of GQDs. (a) 3D fluorescence spectra, (b) fluorescence spectra, (c) CIE coordinate plots, (d) UV–Vis absorption spectra, (e) transient fluorescence lifetime spectra, (f) Tauc curve, (g) schematic of the energy transition mechanism of GQDs, (h) fluorescence spectra at different pH, (i) linear curves of pH and fluorescence intensity.

Fluorescence properties of PHAs/GQDs films. (a) fluorescence intensity with different contents of GQDs, (b) 3D fluorescence mapping, (c) fluorescence spectra, (d) CIE coordinates, (e) fluorescence spectra at different pH conditions, (f) linear curve of pH versus fluorescence intensity, (g) conceptual schematic of the pH detector.

Physical properties of PHAs/GQDs films. (a) stress–strain curves, (b) UV resistance and transparency, (c) digital photographs, TEM images of the cross-sections, corresponding EDS scans of C, N and O elements, (d) POM and 2D-WAXD images.
Antibacterial properties of pure PHAs and PHAs/GQDs nanocomposites. (a) S. aureus. (b) E. coli.

head.ce:title: A novel strategy for the preparation of multifunctional and robust polyhydroxyalkanoates/GQDs nanocomposites with UV-resistance, pH-responsive and antibacterial properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Laboratory of Food Colloids and Biotechnology, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, 1800 Lihu Road, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Food Colloids and Biotechnology
School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Key Laboratory of Food Colloids and Biotechnology, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, 1800 Lihu Road, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biosynthesized polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHAs) offer several compelling advantages, including biodegradability, biocompatibility, and suitability for use in environmentally friendly packaging materials. However, poor crystallization ability and lack of functionality (e.g., UV-resistance and antibacterial properties) significantly restrict the practical applications of PHAs. In this study, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) containing with rich amino groups, carboxy groups and conjugate structure were tailor-made as multifunctional nanofillers to prepare PHAs/GQDs composites. The GQDs not only exhibited excellent dispersion within the PHAs matrix at the nanoscale but also act as nucleating agents to dramatically increase the nucleation density of PHAs. Remarkably, the incorporation of just 0.5 wt% GQDs led to outstanding antibacterial performance (99.99%), UV resistance (90.50%), transparency (>80%) and mechanical properties in the PHAs composite. ​Moreover, these PHAs composites displayed strong fluorescence stability and demonstrated sensitivity to pH variations. Building upon these findings, we propose a novel concept for a wearable device capable of intelligently detecting changes in sweat pH during human exercise. This work presents a straightforward and effective method for preparing multifunctional GQDs, thereby expanding the potential applications of PHAs in the realm of green packaging and intelligent sensing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, plastics produced from fossil fuels set to exceed 390 million tons by 2022 [1]. The dominant use of plastics is packaging, which accounts for almost 13 percent [2]. Only 20 percent of plastic is recycled worldwide effectively [3]. The production and disposal of huge quantities of plastic products have caused a serious energy crisis and environmental pollution. In recent years, driven by government regulations and livelihood needs (e.g. China's explicit efforts to strengthen biomaterials R&D in 2022 and develop green and low-carbon-oriented biomass alternative applications), there has been increasing interest in the non-toxicity, affordability and environmental friendliness of polymers [4,5]. Therefore, the development of green packaging materials is an important issue related to national strategies and livelihood projects.

Polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHAs) are bio-based and bio-degradable polyesters. There are three main methods for producing PHAs, involving microbial fermentation, enzyme catalysis and genetic modification. Microbial fermentation is the most efficient production method [6,7]. Biosynthesized polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHAs) offer several compelling advantages, including biodegradability, biocompatibility, and suitability for use in environmentally friendly packaging materials. So far, PHAs are expensive (more than 40,000 RMB per ton) and only a few companies produce PHAs on a commercial scale (e.g., Kaneka Corporation (Japan), TianAn Biological Materials Co. Ltd. (China)). Finding renewable and low-cost alternatives to reduce production costs is considered the best solution for achieving large-scale production and application of PHAs in the future [8]. In addition, lignocellulosic waste, glycerol, molasses, whey, coffee residue, edible oils, etc. have been used as raw materials for PHAs to reduce costs [9].

Recent developments in polymer nanocomposites (PNC) technology have provided the new solution for the high performance and functionalization of PHAs [10,11]. The common nanofillers mainly include Mxene [12], carbon nanotubes [13], graphene [14], nanoclay [15], zinc oxide (ZnO) [16], titanium dioxide [17], cellulose [18], lignin [19] and so on. A nanofiller usually has only 1–2 special functions. For example, metal oxides such as ZnO are often used as antimicrobial agents to enhance the antimicrobial properties of polymers. However, the incorporation of conventional metal oxides usually leads to degradation of PHAs matrix during melt processing [20,21]. In addition, nanofillers tend to agglomerate in the polymer matrix due to the large specific surface area [20]. Therefore, it is of great significance to realize the high performance and multifunctionality of PHAs by designing a multifunctional non-metallic oxide nanofiller with good compatibility.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials less than 10 nm in size with significant fluorescence properties [22]. As a new luminescent material with tunable photoluminescence, good biocompatibility and low toxicity, GQDs have been used in various medical and optical fields [23–26]. However, poor compatibility between GQDs and polyesters such as PHAs resulted in the tendency for GQDs to agglomerate during processing [27–29]. By adjusting the chemical structure and surface functional groups of GQDs, it is possible to improve the compatibility and optical properties as well as to endow them with multifunctionality, which is expected to expand the applications of GQDs in PHAs [29–32]. Cosgun et al. [30] compounded hydrophilic GQDs with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to prepare transparent PVA/GQD films, which exhibited good optical properties. Actually, most polymers are hydrophobic. Afterwards, Ma et al. [31] successfully doped the developed DFNS@CQDs-MoS2 nanoparticles into hydrophobic polyurethane acrylate (HWPUA), which not only avoided the aggregation of the nanoparticles but also endowed the antibacterial activity of HWPUA. Coffee grounds were used as a raw material to prepare biobased GQDs, which has good compatibility with polylactic acid (PLA) and the PLA/GQDs nanocomposites with excellent UV resistance were obtained by solution blending [32]. Currently, there are fewer studies related to the design of bio-polyesters/GQDs nanocomposites, and the study mainly focuses on how to improve the compatibility of GQDs with polyesters. Integration of multiple functions in the GQDs through structural design, leading to high performance and multifunctionality of bio-polyesters has not been reported yet.

In this study, GQDs containing with rich amino groups, carboxy groups and conjugate structure were tailor-made as multifunctional nanofillers to prepare PHAs/GQDs composites. The GQDs not only exhibited excellent dispersion within the PHAs matrix at the nanoscale but also act as nucleating agents to dramatically increase the nucleation density of PHAs. Meanwhile, the PHAs composite present excellent UV resistance, transparency, pH-responsive, antibacterial properties and mechanical properties. This work presents a straightforward and effective method for preparing multifunctional GQDs, thereby expanding the potential applications of PHAs in the realm of green packaging and intelligent sensing.

Citric acid (CA), tryptophan (Try), pentaethylenehexamine (PEHA) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Polyhydroxyalkanoates (the 3HHx content is 11 mol %, Mw ≈ 590,000 and PDI = 2.5) were purchased from Lanjing Microbial Co., Ltd. All other chemical reagents were analytically pure AR and were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. All materials were used without further purification.

The preparation process of GQDs nanoparticles was shown in Fig. 1 . Firstly, 1.0 g CA and 0.7 g Try was added into ultrapure deionized water. After the formation of a suspension, 0.5 g PEHA were added slowly and stirred violently. Then the suspension was transferred to the autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. The suspension was cooled down to room temperature, and then adjusted to pH = 7 in ultrapure water using a 0.22 μm microporous membrane with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 3 kDa in a dialysis bag for 3 days. Finally, the purified GQDs were obtained by freeze-drying.

Firstly, 3 g of PHAs were dissolved in 50 mL of CHCl3 solution. Then the different amounts of GQDs were dissolved in 4 mL of C2H5OH solution. The GQDs/C2H5OH solution was added dropwise to the PHAs/CHCl3 solution. The blending was moved to a petri dish after fully dissolution and dried at 25 ℃ for 3 days to prepared the PHAs/GQDs composites (Fig. 1). The weight ratios of each GQDs relative to the PHAs were 0.5 %, 1.0 % and 2.0 %. For comparison, pure PHAs were prepared with the same method.

FT-IR spectra were obtained by the Nicolet 6700 spectrometer. The 1H NMR spectra were recorded by the NMR (AVANCE III HD 400 MHz, Switzerland) with DMSO. Raman spectra were recorded by the microscopic confocal Raman spectrometer (inVia, UK). XRD patterns were obtained with an X-ray diffractometer (D8, Germany). The morphology was characterized by AFM (Nanonavi E-Sweep SPM, Seiko Instruments, Japan), TEM (JEOLJEM-2100, Japan) and SEM (S-4800, HITACHI, Japan) coupled with JEM-2100F energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted on the XPS (Axis supra, UK). Photoluminescence Spectroscopy (PL) with excitation wavelengths from 300 nm to 500 nm were measured by the fluorescence spectrometer (FLS980, USA). The pH responsiveness of GQDs was investigated by a fluorescence photometer. Color coordinates (CIE) and 3D fluorescence maps were analyzed and plotted using color calculator software. Transparency and UV resistance were recorded by the UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV3600, Japan). The mechanical properties were tested by the universal testing machine (5967X, USA). The crystal morphology was observed by POM (Axio Scop, Germany). The two-dimensional WAXS measurements were performed on the XEUSS 3.0 bench equipped with a copper internal source (Genix3D). Plate counting method [33] was determined for evaluation of antimicrobial performance (the detailed test methods can be found in the supporting information). Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Escherichia coli (E. coli) were selected as representative gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria to evaluate the antibacterial performance.

As shown in Fig. 2 b and 2c, GQDs had a spherical shape. The AFM images indicated that GQDs exhibited a uniform size distribution, with the particle size distribution ranging from 1.5 nm to 5.5 nm (Fig. 2c' and 2c“). The average size of GQDs was approximately 3.5 nm. In addition, the carbon skeleton of GQDs were confirmed by XRD and Raman spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 2d and 2e. The appearance of D and G bands in Raman spectra and the broad peak diffraction at 22° in XRD indicated that the GQDs were centred on amorphous carbon of the sp2 hybridization type [34].

The structure of GQDs was characterized by 1H NMR, FT-IR and XPS. More clearly, the characteristic absorptions of the functional groups in the spectra of GQDs were consistent with the raw materials (Fig. 2a). For instance, the strong absorption peaks at 3500 cm−1 and 3000 cm−1 was caused by symmetric stretching vibrations of the –OH and –NH groups and the absorption band at 2900 cm−1 by C–H stretching vibrations and the absorption band at 1695 cm−1 by CO stretching vibrations. Nevertheless, there are some differences in the spectra of GQDs compared to raw materials. Typically, the formation of GQDs was accompanied by the production of –CO–NH– and the stretching vibrations of –CO–NH– appeared at 1639 cm−1, 1542 cm−1 and 1337 cm−1 [35,36]. The characteristic hydrogen of the amide bond and conjugated structure appeared in 1H NMR (Fig. S1). The FT-IR and 1H NMR results demonstrated that the surface of GQDs contained functional groups such as amino, hydroxyl, carboxyl and indole groups.

In order to grasp the elemental composition as well as the functional group species, the GQDs were characterized by XPS (Fig. 2f). GQDs had three peaks at 284.8 eV (C1s), 398.7 eV (N1s) and 529.1 eV (O1s), which indicated that the surface of GQDs contained mainly C, N and O [37,38]. The results showed the percentage of atomic, corresponding to C ∼ 76.0 %, N ∼ 6.1 % and O ∼ 17.9 %. The doping of these elements further enriches the surface structure of the GQDs, thus imparting GQDs with excellent luminescence properties. For further understanding of the types of functional groups in GQDs, the characteristic peaks of C1s, N1s and O1s were fitted (Fig. 2g). The spectra of C elements on the surface of GQDs can be fitted to four peaks corresponding to the binding energies of C–C/CC (284.8 eV), C-N/C-O (286.5 eV), CO (288.5 eV) and N–CO–R' (290.9 eV), indicating that the interaction between the C skeletal chain and the N and O elements [39–42]. This was further evidenced by the N1s peaks and O1s peaks. Thus, through the elemental and structural analysis of GQDs, it is fully confirmed that the surface or edge of GQDs were rich in oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups [43].

The 3D fluorescence spectra of GQDs were shown in Fig. 3 a to intuitively understand the fluorescence properties of GQDs nanoparticles. Apparently, it focuses on the characteristic point on the fluorescence spectrum corresponding to an optimal excitation wavelength of 355 nm and an emission wavelength of 490 nm. For visualization, the fluorescence spectra was shown in Fig. 3b and GQDs had the excitation-dependent behavior. When the excitation wavelength increased from 320 nm to 420 nm, the fluorescence intensity tended to increase with a red-shift phenomenon (stokes shift) [44,45]. The electron in the excited state does not leap radiatively to the ground state, but first undergoes vibrational relaxation and internal conversion, all of which consume some energy. At the same time, the electron leaps to the ground state through a series of vibrational relaxations. These causes result in a loss of energy, which is reflected as a red shift in the spectrum [45–48]. The CIE (Commission Internationale de L'eclairage) coordinates were shown in Fig. 3c. The emitting color of GQDs gradually changed from blue to green, which suggested that the fluorescence stability of GQDs in the solution was weak. [30].

The UV–vis of GQDs was tested as shown in Fig. 3d. GQDs had three distinct absorption peaks at 270 nm, 325 nm and 405 nm. The absorption peak of GQDs solution at 270 nm was observed, which was attributed to the indole aromatic structure with the π-π* leap in the CC structure [49]. A broad shoulder peak near 325 nm was mainly caused by the n-π* leap in the CO structure. More interestingly, a broad peak at 405 nm in GQDs solution was attributed to the unique GQDs N-doped structure [50]. The analysis of the UV–vis results demonstrated that GQDs contained many conjugated sp2-hybridised CC structures and an abundance of CO structures.

The transient fluorescence lifetime spectra for GQDs were shown in Fig. 3e and are fitted by the following equation [51].
R
t
= B 1
e
- t /
τ 1
+ B 2
e
- t /
τ 2
+ B 3
e
- t /
τ 3
+ B 4
e
- t /
τ 4
where R(t) denotes the relative intensity of the fluorescence τ1 and τ2 denote the short-lived and long-lived portions. B is a constant and t is the decay time.

The values of τ1 and τ2 were calculated to be 1.02 and 3.63, respectively, with an average τ value of 2.51 ns (Table S1). The presence of τ1 and τ2 indicated that it had two luminescence centers, originating from the π-π* leap in the carbon nucleus and the n-π* leap in the O- and N-containing functional groups, respectively, which together regulated the fluorescence properties of GQDs [52].

The absorption spectra was converted to a Tauc plot according to the following equation [53].
α h γ
1 n
= B
h γ -
E g
where α is the absorption coefficient, h is Planck constant, γ is the incident photon frequency, B is the scaling constant and Eg is the forbidden band width of the semiconductor material. Thereafter, the representative Tauc curve (Fig. 3f) showed that the excited state energy band (Eg ) of GQDs corresponded to 3.53 eV.

The energy transition mechanism of GQDs was shown in Fig. 3g, where the highest energy band filled with electrons is called the valence band, the band at the end filled with electrons is called the conduction band and the band that cannot be filled with electrons is called the forbidden band. When excited with the UV light, the electron (e-) jumps from the valence band (VB) to the higher energy level of the conduction band (CB), forming a hole (h+) in the valence band. The successful doping of N elements in the GQDs framework reduced the Eg , which reduced the forbidden band width and made it easier for radiative leaps to occur, resulting in better fluorescence behavior [28]. The photobleaching resistance of GQDs was additionally examined while under consistent UV irradiation. The maximum fluorescence intensity hardly changed with time in the continuous UV environment, indicating that GQDs had good resistance to photobleaching (Fig. S2).

In addition to excellent fluorescence properties, pH responsiveness of GQDs was also crucial for the potential applications in intelligent detection. The fluorescence intensity of GQDs at different pH conditions was tested at the optimum excitation wavelength of 350 nm as shown in Fig. 3h. The fluorescence emission of GQDs was sensitive to changes in pH due to the presence of abundant functional groups such as amines, hydroxyl groups, carboxyl groups and pyrrole rings [54]. It is worth noting that there was a good linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and pH in the pH range from 2.0 to10.0 (Fig. 3i). Because nitrogen atoms in nitrogen-containing groups such as amino (–NH2+) and imino (–NH-) groups contained lone pairs of electrons, amino and imino groups can easily be protonated to form –NH3+ and –NH- groups under acidic conditions [55]. The graphene sheets and the nitrogen-containing groups at their edges maintained a relatively high electron density. Furthermore, the protonation and the resulting hydrogen bonding can have a greater contribution in the excited state than in the ground state, which makes GQDs susceptible to photoexcitation and intense fluorescence emission. However, under alkaline conditions, the amino and imino groups in GQDs can be deprotonated to form free –NH2+ and –NH-. This deprotonation enhanced the transfer of lone pairs of electrons to the graphene sheets. As a result, the graphene sheets showed an increased electron density, while the electron density of the nitrogen-containing groups decreased. In this case, GQDs were difficult to excite by light and bring about fluorescence quenching. Similar to nitrogen-containing groups, the protonation of oxygen-containing groups such as –OH and –COOH under acidic conditions achieved an increase in fluorescence intensity and deprotonation under basic conditions leaded to a decrease in fluorescence intensity [56,57]. On the other hand, the pH responsiveness of GQDs may be partly attributed to changes in the concentrations of H+ and OH–, which lead to electron leaps in the π-π* and n-π* of GQDs by repopulating or depleting their valence bands [58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence properties and pH responsiveness of PHAs/GQDs film
ce:section.ce:para: The comprehensive understanding of the structure and properties of GQDs has been achieved through this work. However, one issue that still needs to be addressed is the fluorescence quenching caused by the aggregation of GQDs. The fluorescence properties of the PHAs/GQDs films were further investigated (Fig. 4 a). PHAs showed different fluorescence intensities at the optimum excitation wavelength with different contents of GQDs, which was possible to the better dispersion and locking of GQDs in the PHAs matrix. For visualization purposes, the fluorescence spectra were highlighted in the Fig. 4c. Clearly, the excitation-dependent behavior was observed in the fluorescence spectra. The corresponding CIE coordinates were provided in Fig. 4d. Within the excitation wavelength range from 360 nm to 450 nm, the color of PHAs/GQDs appeared cyan, which indicating that GQDs had strong fluorescence stability in PHAs matrix.

The fluorescence intensity of the PHAs/GQDs films was examined in the pH range from 3 to 9 (Fig. 4e and 4f). Consistent with the pH responsiveness of the GQDs, the fluorescence intensity decreased with increasing pH, showing the desired pH responsiveness. Sweat is an easily accessible body fluid that contains other proteins and metabolites associated with diseases and infections and can provide important diagnostic information [59,60]. The pH level of sweat is another indicator of health. The pH level of human sweat is strictly distributed between 4 and 8. PHAs/GQDs films with sensitive pH responsiveness and excellent mechanical properties have good potential to perform intelligent pH detection of human sweat. As a proof of concept, a wearable pH detector assembled from the PHAs/GQDs films is proposed for human health monitoring. A schematic diagram of the wearable pH detector was shown in Fig. 4g. Its main components consisted of a UV light emitter, a collector and a pH detection. This wearable pH detector had potential for application to monitor human health by sensitively detecting pH changes of sweat during exercise.

As the green packaging and intelligent detection materials, excellent mechanical properties are essential. As shown in Fig. 5 a and 5b, the pure PHAs have a tensile strength of 26 MPa, elongation at break of 435 % and high transparency of 85 % (Fig. 5a and 5b). With the addition of 0.5 wt% GQDs, the tensile strength of PHAs composites increased to 32 MPa and the elongation at break increased to 505 %, meanwhile maintaining high transparency (81 %). This is mainly due to the good dispersion of GQDs in the PHAs matrix (Fig. 5c). The particle size of GQDs was about 30 nm and the C, N and O elements were also uniformly dispersed in the matrix, with no obvious agglomeration. Moreover, the addition of GQDs greatly increased the nucleation density of PHAs and reduced spherulite size (Fig. 5d). As a result, the tensile strength and toughness of the PHAs were improved, while maintaining a high level of transparency. More importantly, with the addition of only 0.5 wt% of GQDs, the UV resistance of PHAs was increased to 90.5 %, which is mainly due to a series of UV absorption and energy conversion processes of the carboxyl, hydroxyl and carbonyl groups in GQDs. The GQDs allow for the accommodation of abundant functional groups and a large number of GQDs-PHAs interfacial phases at the surface and edges, making it easier for the composite to reflect and scatter UV light [61,62]. The UV resistance ability of the GQDs is superior to that of conventional metal oxides and does not lead to oxidative degradation of the polyester during processing and thus deterioration of the mechanical properties of the material [63].

The antibacterial properties of PHAs/GQDs films against gram-negative (E. coli) and gram-positive (S. aureus) were evaluated and the results were shown in Fig. 6 . The viable counts of E. coli were 2.2 × 104 CFU/cm2 and S. aureus were 2.1 × 104 CFU/cm2 after 0 h of inoculation for pure PHAs. The viable counts of E. coli were 2.3 × 105 CFU/cm2 and S. aureus were 6.3 × 103 CFU/cm2 after 24 h of inoculation for pure PHAs. The viable counts of the PHAs/GQDs-0.5 films had a viable count of 1 CFU/cm2 for E. coli and 1 CFU/cm2 for S. aureus after 24 h of inoculation. With the addition of 0.5 wt% GQDs, the antibacterial rate of PHAs/GQDs for E. coli reached 99.99 % and the antibacterial rate of PHAs/GQDs for S. aureus reached 99.98 %. In addition, the MIC and MBC of GQDs were tested as shown in supporting information. The GQDs have a better antibacterial effect against S. aureus and E. coli. For S. aureus, the MIC was 1 mg/mL and the MBC was 2.5 mg/mL. For E. coli, the MIC was 5 mg/mL and the MBC was 10 mg/mL (Fig. S3). In this work, the N-containing GQDs with amides and amines are protonated in aqueous solution. This enhances GQDs electrostatic interaction with the negatively charged components of the lipid membrane, which can cause disruption to many aspects of bacterial physiology (such as nucleotides, lipids, proteins and metabolites) and ultimately leads to cell death [64]. The antibacterial properties of GQDs are better than the traditional antibacterial agents (e.g., ZnO, TiO2) [65,66]. Therefore, PHAs/GQDs composites prepared in this work have great prospects for the green packaging (Table S2).

Multifunctional GQDs were synthesized from citric acid, tryptophan and pentaethylenehexamine with an easy and low-cost method to obtain environmentally friendly PHAs/GQDs composites. The GQDs exhibited excellent dispersion within the PHAs matrix at the nanoscale, which solves the problem of poor compatibility between hydrophobic polyesters and GQDs. The incorporation of 0.5 wt% GQDs led to excellent antibacterial performance, UV resistance, transparency, and mechanical properties in the PHAs composite. Moreover, the PHAs composite has a sensitive pH responsive and the fluorescence intensity varies with the pH stimulus. Building upon these findings, we propose the concept of a wearable device for intelligent detection of changes in sweat pH during human exercise and monitoring of human health. This work presents a straightforward and effective method for preparing multifunctional GQDs, thereby expanding the potential application of PHAs in the realm of green packaging and intelligent sensing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52103032, 52073123), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20200606), Distinguished Young Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20200027), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2021M691267), Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX23_2557).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.147106.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Are bioplastics an ecofriendly alternative to fossil fuel plastics?

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Ali, H. Ali, S. Souissi, P. Zinck, Are bioplastics an ecofriendly alternative to fossil fuel plastics? Environ. Chem. Lett. 21 (2023) 1991-2002.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Production, use, and fate of all plastics ever made

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Plastic food packaging: perceptions and attitudes of Portuguese consumers about environmental impact and recycling
